front: Figure: nicknamed "Milk Snatcher" after abolishing a free school milk program as Education Secretary under Edward Heath
Figure: became Conservative Party leader in 1975 by defeating Edward Heath
Figure: before entering politics, worked as a research chemist and wrote a dissertation on the structure of the antibiotic peptide gramicidin S
Figure: as a Conservative MP, voted in favor of the Sexual Offences Act (1967), which partially decriminalized homosexuality
Figure: won the 1979 general election, defeating James Callaghan after the strike-plagued "Winter of Discontent"
Figure: her 1979 campaign used a Saatchi & Saatchi poster showing a long unemployment line with the slogan that the other party "wasn't working"
Event: led Britain through this 1982 war against Argentina after Argentine forces invaded the namesake islands
Figure: Labour's Ken Livingstone criticized her decision to send British troops to the Falklands
Event: broke this year-long strike by refusing any concessions over pit closures, aided by National Coal Board chairman Ian MacGregor, and called the strikers "the enemy within"
Event: mounted police clashed violently with picketers here during the miners' strike
Law: reinstituted this act in response to the 1984-85 miners' strike
Law: this flat local tax, derisively called the "poll tax," triggered 1990 riots and led to her ouster as Conservative leader
work: Domestic Policy: Poll Tax, Section 28 & Social Reform

front: Law: this anti-LGBT provision of the Local Government Act sparked a 25,000-person protest led by John Shiers
work: Domestic Policy: Poll Tax, Section 28 & Social Reform

front: Event: her white paper "Streamlining the Cities" abolished these county-level bodies, including the GLC led by "Red" Ken Livingstone, sparking the "rate-capping rebellion"
work: Domestic Policy: Poll Tax, Section 28 & Social Reform

front: Law: her government's first of two Housing Acts introduced this type of tenancy
work: Domestic Policy: Poll Tax, Section 28 & Social Reform

front: Document: her government commissioned this report following the Brixton riots
work: Domestic Policy: Poll Tax, Section 28 & Social Reform

front: Event: a memo sent to her after this riot blamed the unrest on "bad moral attitudes" instead of racism
work: Domestic Policy: Poll Tax, Section 28 & Social Reform

front: Document: through these two white papers, she reformed the NHS by creating an internal market
back: "Caring for People" and "Working for Patients" (Margaret Thatcher)
work: Domestic Policy: Poll Tax, Section 28 & Social Reform

front: Figure: declared in 1987 that "there is no such thing as society," in the context of personal responsibility
work: Domestic Policy: Poll Tax, Section 28 & Social Reform

front: Event: this 1984 attack on the Grand Hotel in Brighton, carried out by IRA member Patrick Magee, was an attempt to kill her during the Conservative Party conference
Work: novel in which conspirator "Dan," using the alias Roy Walsh, checks into the Grand Hotel (where Freya Finch works) to prepare the Brighton bombing
Work: 2014 short story collection whose title story depicts an IRA operative's fictional killing of Margaret Thatcher, ending with the line "Rejoice, fucking rejoice"
back: The Assassination of Margaret Thatcher (Hilary Mantel)

front: Work: novel in which a cocaine-using Nick Guest dances with Margaret Thatcher at a party hosted by the Feddens
Work: novel in which Chamcha nicknames Margaret Thatcher "Torture," whose effigy is often burned at the Club Hot Wax
Work: novel with a scene in which a translated conversation has a Spanish man ask Margaret Thatcher "do the people in your country love you?" instead of relaying her offer of tea
Work: musical in which Margaret Thatcher is sarcastically wished "Merry Christmas" at the start of Act II, and the protagonist's father is later attacked as a "scab" during the miners' strike
Work: sitcom scene in which Leslie Knope role-plays as Margaret Thatcher, joking about losing trust in Ronald Reagan after his invasion of Grenada
